coverage_affirmations.gitleaks_explanation: gitleaks not installed; install to cover secrets-in-history

entries.title: Governance single-point-of-failure on a privileged keyboard-interception daemon with active release cadence (C20)
entries.action_hint: Pin to a reviewed release tag; run kanata as a dedicated user with minimum-needed input-device privileges.
entries.threat_model_context: kanata is a privileged input daemon — it runs with elevated OS-level privileges to intercept keystrokes and re-emit remapped events. The user's entire keyboard input passes through the daemon, which means: (a) a compromised kanata binary = a keylogger with exfil opportunity; (b) a compromised kanata binary + the `--features cmd` shell-exec feature = local RCE triggered by keystroke. The supply chain path is: commit → `cargo install kanata` pulls from crates.io, or GitHub Releases ships prebuilt binaries. No branch protection, no rulesets, no CODEOWNERS (E2). Recent release cadence (v1.12.0-prerelease-2 on 2026-04-12, 8 days before scan) means code lands with users fast (E4) — which is why c20 fires Critical here rather than Warning (compare to wezterm entry 21 where the release stall downgraded the same compound to Warning).
entries.what_this_means: A compromise of jtroo's GitHub or crates.io publisher account reaches every user's keyboard-input stream on the next prerelease. The review gate is voluntary (see F3 for the positive angle), but structurally absent.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of current compromise. 44% formal-review-rate (E3) is the highest in the V1.2 catalog and is a real positive signal — just not enforced via tooling. The Critical call is about the ABSENCE of enforced structural review, not the ABSENCE of review activity.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: enable a ruleset requiring ≥1 approving review on main; add CODEOWNERS. The existing voluntary-review culture (44% formal rate) suggests this would be low-friction to adopt — protection infrastructure would codify existing practice. Consumer-side: pin to specific version tag + review changelogs; consider running kanata as a dedicated system user with CAP_NET_ADMIN rather than root on Linux.

entries.title: No SECURITY.md + 0 published advisories in 4 years on a keyboard-interception daemon
entries.action_hint: If you find a kanata bug, use GitHub's `Report a vulnerability` button — not documented but available.
entries.threat_model_context: community/profile reports has_security_policy=false. security-advisories count: 0 across 4 years. kanata handles every keystroke with elevated privileges on all three major OS platforms — silent-fix cadence on a tool of this class puts downstream consumers in the dark about which releases carry security-relevant fixes. Consistent with the catalog-wide pattern (Kronos / Xray-core / wezterm / QuickLook all show the same 0-advisory posture despite privileged-tool shape).
entries.what_this_means: GHSA syndication does not flow from kanata. Release notes are the only channel for learning about security-relevant changes.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of suppressed vulns. The active maintenance + healthy review rate make silent-fix-via-release the plausible explanation.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add a 1-page SECURITY.md naming GitHub's private-advisory button. Given kanata's privileged threat model, this is especially cheap/valuable.

entries.title: No Dependabot config — Rust workspace with 15+ Cargo.toml files has no automated CVE-watch on dep graph
entries.action_hint: Run `cargo audit` locally after building; the repo doesn't do it for you.
entries.threat_model_context: dependabot.yml is absent (E9). The Cargo workspace has 15+ manifests spanning parser, tcp_protocol, keyberon keyboard layout engine, interception (Windows driver binding), WASM target, and 8 more sub-crates. Transitive deps include serde, tokio, and platform-specific input libraries. None of these receive automated CVE alerts — the maintainer has to discover security-relevant dep updates manually. Same gap as wezterm (entry 21, 70+ Cargo crates unwatched).
entries.what_this_means: A CVE against a core Rust dep in kanata's graph (e.g., a serialize-parser bug in serde's JSON layer used by the tcp_protocol crate) would not surface automatically.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of a current reachable CVE — harness osv.dev query returned 0 (F5 coverage gap; Cargo parsing not implemented).
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add `.github/dependabot.yml` with `- package-ecosystem: cargo` — minutes of work. Consumer-side: `cargo audit` against the installed Cargo.lock before trusting a self-build.

entries.title: 44% formal PR review rate — highest in the V1.2 catalog; active two-maintainer pair
entries.action_hint: Positive signal worth noting; context for reading F0's Critical verdict.
entries.threat_model_context: Across a 300-PR sample, 44% of PRs carry formal `APPROVED` review decisions — substantially higher than Xray-core (3.3%), kamal (4.7%), QuickLook (5.1%), wezterm (3.3%), browser_terminal (0%). jtroo (979 commits) + ItayGarin (149 commits) are an active two-maintainer pair; external contributor PRs routinely go through formal review before merge. The 117 self-merges (39%) are predominantly jtroo's own direct-commits — an expected pattern for the primary author. This is the healthiest review culture in the V1.2 catalog so far — it's just not codified in branch-protection rules (F0 + E2).
entries.what_this_means: The governance gap in F0 is structural (no enforcement) but not behavioral (enforcement would codify existing practice). Low-friction fix path.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Doesn't substitute for branch protection. A compromised account bypasses voluntary review entirely. The positive signal is that adding protection would be easy — the reviews are already happening.
entries.date_label: Ongoing — pattern visible in recent PRs
entries.how_to_fix: No fix required on the positive-signal side. The action is for F0 (codify the existing review behavior into a ruleset).

entries.title: `cmd` feature flag enables config-level shell execution — opt-in risk-expansion
entries.action_hint: If you don't need config-triggered shell commands, install without `--features cmd` (the default).
entries.threat_model_context: kanata supports an optional `cmd` feature (`cargo install kanata --features cmd`) that lets config-file actions execute arbitrary shell commands. Feature-gating is deliberate risk-reduction — by default, kanata is key-remapping-only with no shell-exec capability. A user who opts into `cmd` has explicitly expanded their daemon's capability to include 'run commands on my machine triggered by keystrokes'. Config files become executable content, which is a different threat model than pure key-remap.
entries.what_this_means: For default installs (without `cmd`): threat model is keylogger-grade (worst case). For `cmd`-feature installs: threat model also includes arbitrary local command execution from any kanata config loaded, including configs shared/copied from the internet.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a vulnerability — feature-gated + documented + opt-in is the right pattern. The finding exists to make the capability explicit to readers.
entries.how_to_fix: N/A — the design is appropriate. Consumer guidance: treat kanata configs with `cmd` actions as executable code; only load configs you audited.

entries.title: Rust-ecosystem scanner gaps — Cargo.lock parsing + workspace sub-crate channel misidentification
entries.action_hint: Same gap pattern as wezterm (entry 21); consumer-side `cargo audit` recommended.
entries.threat_model_context: Two repeated gaps: (1) Cargo.lock parsing not implemented — runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 despite 15+ Cargo.toml files; (2) distribution-channel harness surfaced 12 crates.io channels from workspace sub-crates when only `crates-kanata` is the genuine install path. Same gap pattern as wezterm; the pattern now spans 2 Rust entries in the catalog. Dangerous-primitives small hits (1/1/1 across deserialization/secrets/tls_cors) are consistent with the Rust-serde / CIDR false-positive class.
entries.what_this_means: For this scan, Rust dep-CVE surface is not assessed. Combined with F2 (no dependabot), the maintainer side also doesn't have automated dep-CVE surfacing.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a kanata defect — a property of the scanner that should be closed in a V1.2.x harness patch.
entries.how_to_fix: Scanner-side: Cargo.lock parsing + RustSec advisory-db lookup; disambiguate workspace sub-crates from publishable channels by checking Cargo.toml `[package]` presence + crates.io registry confirmation.

entries.claim: kanata is a cross-platform keyboard-remapping daemon. Privileged on every platform: Linux uses uinput (requires CAP_NET_ADMIN or root); macOS uses IOKit/HID (often requires root + Accessibility/Input-Monitoring permissions); Windows uses the Interception driver (requires admin install). Intercepts every keystroke in real time.
entries.command: gh api repos/jtroo/kanata --jq '{description, topics}'; head README.md
entries.result: description: 'Improve keyboard comfort and usability with advanced customization'. topics: [cross-platform, interception-driver, keyboard, keyboard-layout, linux, macos]. Runtime model: a daemon runs with elevated privileges, captures all keyboard input, applies user-configured remapping rules (layers, tap-hold, macros), and emits remapped keystrokes to the OS. The user's raw keystrokes flow through the daemon.

entries.claim: No branch protection — classic HTTP 404, 0 rulesets, 0 rules on default branch, no CODEOWNERS. Relies entirely on voluntary review discipline.
entries.result: classic: HTTP 404. rulesets: {entries: [], count: 0}. rules_on_default: {entries: [], count: 0}. CODEOWNERS: not found at any standard path.

entries.claim: PR review sample (300 PRs): formal_review_rate=44.0%, any_review_rate=48.0%, self_merge_count=117 (39%). **Formal review rate is the HIGHEST in the V1.2 catalog** — despite no branch-protection enforcement, jtroo + ItayGarin run a disciplined review culture for external contributions.
entries.result: sample_size: 300; formal_review_rate: 44.0 (132 of 300 PRs carry `APPROVED` review decisions); any_review_rate: 48.0; self_merge_count: 117. Pattern from sampling recent PRs: external contributor PRs routinely show `review_decision=APPROVED` + 1 reviewer; maintainer's own PRs (jtroo) are frequently self-merged without review. This is a genuine two-tier review pattern, not signal noise.

entries.claim: Active semver-prerelease cadence — v1.12.0-prerelease-2 on 2026-04-12 (8 days before scan). 58 total releases; recent push velocity on main is 2026-04-19 (day before scan).
entries.result: total_count: 58. Latest: v1.12.0-prerelease-2 (2026-04-12). Repo pushed_at: 2026-04-19 (7 days later; 1 day before scan). Active development + active release cadence — the code IS reaching users as it lands.

entries.claim: Cargo workspace with 15 manifest files across 12 publishable-looking sub-crates. Main distribution is `cargo install kanata` (on crates.io) or GitHub Releases binary + platform-specific installers.
entries.command: gh api repos/jtroo/kanata/contents | jq '.[] | select(.type == "dir") | .name'; gh api repos/jtroo/kanata/contents/Cargo.toml | jq -r .content | base64 -d | head
entries.result: Cargo workspace members: parser, example_tcp_client, tcp_protocol, key-sort-add, windows_key_tester, keyberon, keyberon-macros, interception, simulated_input, simulated_passthru, wasm. README documents: `cargo install kanata` (basic), `cargo install kanata --features cmd` (shell-exec feature opt-in), `cargo install kanata --features interception_driver` (Windows). Also GitHub Releases prebuilt binaries.

entries.claim: community/profile: health 57%; no SECURITY.md, no CONTRIBUTING, no CODE_OF_CONDUCT. security-advisories count: 0 across 4 years of development.
entries.result: health_percentage: 57; has_security_policy: false; has_contributing: false; has_code_of_conduct: false; license_spdx: LGPL-3.0. security-advisories count: 0. For a keyboard-interception daemon handling every keystroke, 0-advisories-in-4-years points at silent-fix cadence (consistent with catalog-wide pattern) not bug-freeness.

entries.claim: Contributor distribution: jtroo (984), ItayGarin (149), rszyma (47), eugenesvk (38), malpern (27). Top-1 share 79% — below the 80% solo-maintainer threshold. ItayGarin is a substantial co-maintainer at 149 commits (~12% share).
entries.result: jtroo: 984; ItayGarin: 149; rszyma: 47; eugenesvk: 38; malpern: 27; next: smaller tail. total_contributor_count: 100. Top-1 alone at 79% (just below the is_solo_maintainer threshold of 80%). Combined top-2 share ~91%.

entries.claim: Five GitHub Actions workflows cover Linux/macOS/Windows builds + cargo-checks + build-everything meta. No CodeQL, no Dependabot. CI depth is solid for a cross-platform Rust binary.
entries.result: 5 workflows: build-everything.yml, linux-build.yml, macos-build.yml, windows-build.yml, rust.yml (cargo-checks). pull_request_target_count: 0. Good coverage of the three OS targets + lint/test pipeline.

entries.claim: No Dependabot config (dependabot.yml absent). Rust workspace with 15+ Cargo manifests has no automated CVE-watch on Cargo dep graph. Same-shape gap as wezterm (entry 21).
entries.result: HTTP 404. dependabot_config.present: false. No Dependabot ecosystems tracked. The 15+ Cargo.toml files have transitive dep graphs that include serde, tokio, and the platform-specific input libraries (uinput, IOKit bindings, Interception driver) — none automatically CVE-watched.

entries.claim: Active feature-gated shell-execution capability — `cargo install kanata --features cmd` enables the `cmd` action that runs shell commands from kanata config. Feature-gated means opt-in; off by default.
entries.command: gh api repos/jtroo/kanata/contents/README.md | jq -r .content | base64 -d | grep -A2 'features cmd'
entries.result: README documents the `cmd` feature: 'cargo install kanata --features cmd' enables a config-level action to execute shell commands. Feature-gated is a deliberate risk-reduction — by-default kanata does not execute arbitrary commands. Users who opt in are informed.

entries.claim: Distribution channels: `cargo install kanata` via crates.io (primary), GitHub Releases prebuilt binaries for 3 platforms, and Arch Linux AUR + Homebrew + MS Store are documented third-party channels. Scanner detected 12 'crates-*' channels (all 12 workspace sub-crates) — only `crates-kanata` is the genuine install channel.
entries.result: GitHub Releases binaries: kanata-linux, kanata-macos (Intel + Apple Silicon), kanata_winLib.exe, kanata_winIoKey.exe, kanata_winWin.exe (multiple Windows variants for the 3 supported input-capture backends). Unsigned binaries — no .sig, no .asc, no .sha256. Crates.io install path provides its own integrity story (crates.io index hashes).

entries.claim: Harness Cargo.lock parsing not implemented; 12 workspace sub-crates were surfaced as crates.io distribution channels when only the main `kanata` crate is the actual consumer install path. Same coverage-gap pattern as wezterm (entry 21). Dangerous-primitives hits (deserialization 1, secrets 1, tls_cors 1) are likely false positives on Rust serde idioms / Cargo.toml strings / network-CIDR patterns.
entries.result: dependencies.runtime_count: 0 (Cargo-parsing gap). distribution_channels.channels: 12 entries, all from workspace sub-crates — real channels (crates.io main + GitHub Releases binaries + AUR + Homebrew + MS Store) not recognized. dangerous_primitives: 3 total hits — small, consistent with Rust-serde / test-fixture false-positive class.

section_01.pill_row: 7k⭐ · LGPL-3.0 · Rust · 1 critical · 2 warnings · 3 info · CRITICAL
section_01.summary: kanata is a 7k-star Rust keyboard remapper / key-interception daemon by jtroo + co-maintainer ItayGarin. Runs with elevated OS privileges on Linux (uinput), macOS (IOKit), and Windows (Interception driver) — every keystroke flows through it. Critical verdict via C20: no branch protection / rulesets / CODEOWNERS on a keylogger-class privileged tool, paired with active release cadence (v1.12.0-prerelease-2 shipped 8 days before scan). But the governance picture is mixed — F3 is a positive finding: 44% formal PR review rate is the highest in the V1.2 catalog, and the two-maintainer pair demonstrably runs a disciplined voluntary review process. The Critical call is about ABSENCE of enforced structural review, not ABSENCE of review activity.

section_02.pill_row: F0 Critical · F1 Warning · F2 Warning · F3 Info (positive) · F4 Info · F5 Info

section_06.summary: **Zero scorecard overrides** on this scan — all 4 Phase 4 colors match Phase 3 advisory. Third V1.2 wild scan with 0 overrides (after wezterm entry 21 + QuickLook entry 22). Q1 AMBER (not red!) via the 44% formal review rate — first amber Q1 on a catalog entry without a Q1 override. Q2 green (active release cadence + 0 open security issues). Q3 red (no SECURITY.md + 0 advisories). Q4 amber (crates.io integrity + GitHub Releases unsigned binaries + privileged threat model).

split_axis_decision.reasoning: Single consumer profile. Optional `cmd` feature flag is a configuration choice at install time, not a deployment-context variation — F4 captures that risk-expansion without needing split-axis treatment.

groups.headline: Critical — F0 drives the verdict via C20 + active release cadence
groups.one_line_summary: Privileged keyboard-interception daemon + no branch protection + code ships to users fast.

items.claim: Keylogger-class privileged tool with no branch protection / rulesets / CODEOWNERS; latest prerelease 8 days before scan.

groups.headline: Warning — F1 + F2 describe disclosure + dep-hygiene gaps

items.claim: No SECURITY.md + 0 published advisories in 4 years on an input-intercepting daemon.
No Dependabot config — Cargo workspace of 15+ manifests has no automated CVE watch.

groups.headline: Info — F3 (positive) + F4 + F5
groups.one_line_summary: Healthy review culture, feature-gated shell-exec, scanner coverage gaps.

items.claim: **Positive**: 44% formal PR review rate — highest in V1.2 catalog. Two-maintainer pair with genuine review discipline.
`cmd` feature flag (opt-in) enables config-level shell execution — documented + off by default.
Rust-ecosystem scanner gaps (Cargo.lock parsing + workspace channel detection). Same as wezterm.

selections.reason: If branch protection is enabled (which would just codify F3's existing voluntary review culture), F0 collapses from Critical to Warning. One maintainer-side ruleset change closes the single highest-severity finding.
The 44% formal-review rate is the positive counterweight to F0. Falsification (e.g., if the rate dropped substantially on a future scan) would make F3 disappear and amplify F0's severity reading.
Adding dependabot.yml is a one-commit change that closes F2. Low-cost high-value fix; its absence despite other CI investment is worth calling out.

entries.headline: Install via `cargo install kanata` — DO NOT enable `--features cmd` unless you need shell-exec actions
entries.non_technical: The default kanata install is key-remap-only. Only add `--features cmd` if you specifically need your kanata config to run shell commands, and only load configs you trust.

entries.headline: Pin to a specific released version — avoid `cargo install kanata --git` tracking main
entries.non_technical: Pin to the latest released semver (e.g., v1.12.0) rather than building from main. The 44% formal-review rate (F3) applies to merged-to-main code, but you still want the release gate.

entries.headline: Run `cargo audit` against the installed binary's dep graph
entries.non_technical: Because Dependabot is absent (F2), the Cargo dep graph has no automated CVE watch. Cargo-audit surfaces RustSec advisory-db entries locally.

entries.headline: Run kanata as a dedicated user with minimum-needed input privileges — not as root
entries.non_technical: On Linux, kanata needs CAP_NET_ADMIN for uinput (not full root). Create a dedicated `kanata` user with just that capability. On macOS, grant Input Monitoring + Accessibility to the kanata.app specifically. On Windows, the Interception driver install requires admin but the daemon itself can run in a lower-privilege context.

entries.headline: Audit any kanata configs you download from the internet before loading
entries.non_technical: kanata configs are a DSL that maps keystrokes to actions. If your install has `--features cmd` enabled, configs can run shell commands — so a shared config file is executable content. Read it before loading.

executable_file_inventory.lead: kanata ships as a single daemon binary per platform — `kanata` on Linux/macOS, `kanata.exe` on Windows with 3 variants for the different input-capture backends (Interception driver, IoKey, raw Windows hooks). The binary runs with elevated OS privileges to intercept and emit keyboard events.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: Primary surface is the kanata daemon process. Cargo workspace sub-crates (parser, tcp_protocol, keyberon, interception binding, WASM target, etc.) are linked into the single binary or exposed as auxiliary tools.

layer2_entries.runtime: Rust (with libc / IOKit / Windows API bindings via platform-specific sub-crates)
layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: Raw keyboard-device I/O (read keystrokes, write remapped events). Elevated OS privileges required on all 3 platforms.
layer2_entries.network: None by default. Optional `tcp` feature adds a local-only TCP server for control.
layer2_entries.notes: The privileged core. Every keystroke flows through this binary; compromise = keylogger + optional RCE (if `cmd` feature enabled — F4).

Parses kanata's custom Lisp-ish config format. User-authored configs become kanata runtime state — parser bugs here could affect remapping behavior but not directly RCE unless config-level actions include `cmd`.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: Kernel-level keyboard-input driver interaction (Interception is a kernel driver that kanata talks to via user-mode DLL).
layer2_entries.notes: Only compiled into Windows builds when `--features interception_driver` is enabled. The underlying Interception driver itself is third-party and must be separately installed by the user.

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: Install paths: `cargo install kanata` (+ optional features cmd/interception_driver/tcp); prebuilt GitHub Releases binaries; AUR package (Arch); Homebrew formula (macOS); Microsoft Store. Cargo-install integrity is whatever crates.io provides; GitHub-Releases binaries are unsigned.

pr_sample_review.intro: Representative rows from the 300-PR sample. Full sample: 44% formal review rate (highest in V1.2 catalog), 48% any-review, 117 self-merges (39%). Pattern: external-contributor PRs routinely have formal APPROVED decisions; maintainer's own PRs go direct without review — a healthy two-tier model.

entries: Rust Cargo.lock parsing not implemented — 15+ Cargo manifests with transitive dep graphs are unscanned. Same gap as wezterm (entry 21); consistent Rust-ecosystem issue.

Distribution-channel harness surfaced 12 crates.io channels from Cargo workspace sub-crates — only `crates-kanata` is the real install channel. GitHub Releases, AUR, Homebrew, MS Store channels all unrecognized.
Dependabot alerts API required admin scope (HTTP 403); dependabot.yml does not exist in the repo — no ecosystem coverage at all (F2).
Dangerous-primitives regex false-positives on Rust idioms (deserialization hits on serde impls — same pattern as wezterm).
Gitleaks secret-scanning not available on this scanner host.
OSSF Scorecard not indexed — governance signals derived from raw gh api data.
Release-integrity grading is coarse — harness doesn't inspect GitHub Releases assets for .sig / code-signing / .sha256 on kanata binaries.

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: Partly — 44% formal PR review (highest in V1.2 catalog) + active 2-maintainer pair, but no branch protection enforcement

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: Yes — 0 open security issues, 58 releases, latest 8 days before scan (v1.12.0-prerelease-2)

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: No — no SECURITY.md, no CONTRIBUTING, 0 published advisories in 4 years on a privileged input daemon

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: Partly — `cargo install kanata` integrity via crates.io; GitHub Releases binaries unsigned; privileged-tool threat model

catalog_metadata.short_description: Rust cross-platform keyboard remapper / key-interception daemon. Layers, tap-hold, macros, config-driven remapping. Linux uinput, macOS IOKit, Windows Interception-driver backends.
catalog_metadata.target_user: Developer / power-user who wants advanced keyboard customization (home-row mods, tap-hold, layers) beyond what the OS provides. Install: `cargo install kanata` OR GitHub Releases binary (platform-specific). Runs as elevated daemon.
catalog_metadata.shape: Rust CLI daemon + multi-platform input-interception (kernel-adjacent privileges) + Cargo workspace with 12 sub-crates. NEW shape — first keyboard-remapper / input-daemon category in catalog; second Rust V1.2 entry (after wezterm).
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: None — first scan of kanata. Eighth wild V1.2-schema scan (after markitdown 15, ghostty 16, Kronos 17, kamal 18, Xray-core 19, browser_terminal 20, wezterm 21, QuickLook 22).

phase_4_structured_llm.timeline_intro: kanata lifecycle — 4-year jtroo-led project with ItayGarin as active co-maintainer; active semver-prerelease cadence through 2026.
phase_4_structured_llm.evidence_intro: Command-backed evidence from Phase 1 harness + gh api + workspace inspection + README reading.
phase_4_structured_llm.coverage_intro: Phase 1 harness coverage with 7 documented gaps — concentrated on Rust-ecosystem parsing (Cargo.lock, workspace sub-crate disambiguation). Same pattern as wezterm (entry 21).

editorial_caption.text: kanata — 7k-star Rust keyboard-remapper daemon by jtroo + ItayGarin. Runs with elevated privileges on Linux/macOS/Windows; every keystroke flows through it. Critical verdict via C20: keylogger-class privileged tool + no branch protection + active 8-day release cadence. But the governance story is nuanced — **44% formal PR review** is the highest in the V1.2 catalog; the two-maintainer pair runs disciplined voluntary review. The fix for F0 is codifying what's already happening.

body_paragraphs: kanata runs as a daemon with elevated OS privileges on all three major platforms — CAP_NET_ADMIN + uinput on Linux, IOKit + Accessibility + Input-Monitoring on macOS, and the Interception kernel driver on Windows. The reason is mechanical: to remap keys you have to intercept the raw keystream before the OS delivers it to applications, which requires kernel-adjacent access. The consequence is that a compromised kanata binary is a keylogger with exfiltration opportunity — and if the user opted into `--features cmd` (F4), also a local-RCE triggered by arbitrary keystrokes.

The supply-chain path goes through crates.io (`cargo install kanata`) or GitHub Releases prebuilt binaries. The repo has no branch protection, no rulesets on the default branch, no CODEOWNERS. Recent release cadence is active: v1.12.0-prerelease-2 shipped 2026-04-12, 8 days before scan, and main was pushed to the day before scan. The code IS reaching users — which is why the c20 compound rule fires Critical here rather than the Warning it produced for wezterm (entry 21) where the 807-day release stall blunted the same structural gap.

The positive angle is F3: the 44% formal-review rate on PRs is real and the highest in the catalog. jtroo and ItayGarin demonstrably run a voluntary review process for external contributions. The fix for F0 isn't 'build a review culture' — that already exists. The fix is codifying it: enable a ruleset that requires ≥1 approving review before merge to main. That would take the existing behavior and make it structurally unavoidable, closing the account-compromise attack path.

Consumer-side mitigation: pin to a specific released version (not a main-tracking `cargo install --git`); run kanata as a dedicated user with only the specific OS capability it needs (CAP_NET_ADMIN on Linux, not root); if you don't need shell-exec from configs, don't enable `--features cmd`.

community/profile reports has_security_policy=false. security-advisories count: 0 over 4 years. kanata processes every keystroke on every user's machine — the threat model calls for clear disclosure infrastructure. Zero advisories in this context almost certainly means silent-fix cadence, where fixes ship in the next release without formal GHSA publication. Same catalog-wide pattern observed on Kronos (entry 17), Xray-core (entry 19), wezterm (entry 21), QuickLook (entry 22).

For consumers, the effect is that watching the GitHub Security Advisory feed tells you nothing about kanata fixes. Reading release notes is the only channel. For reporters, GitHub's `Report a vulnerability` button is reachable but not documented. A 1-page SECURITY.md would close the reporter-side gap at minimal cost.

There is no `.github/dependabot.yml` in this repo — `gh api contents/.github/dependabot.yml` returns 404. The Cargo workspace has 15+ manifests spanning the main `kanata` crate, parser, tcp_protocol, keyberon (keyboard-layout engine), interception (Windows driver binding), WASM target, and 8 more sub-crates. Transitive deps include serde, tokio, and platform-specific input libraries. None of these receive automated CVE alerts.

Same gap we saw on wezterm (entry 21) — the Rust-ecosystem Dependabot-coverage pattern is consistent across the catalog's Rust entries. The fix is a single YAML file: `- package-ecosystem: cargo` with daily schedule. Low-cost, high-value — especially on a privileged-tool repo where a transitive-dep CVE could propagate into keylogger-scope impact.

Looking at recent PRs: #2032 APPROVED by jtroo, #2031 APPROVED, #2029 APPROVED, #2028 APPROVED — a run of 8 consecutive externally-authored PRs all carrying `review_decision: APPROVED` before merge. Then #2022 and #2021 are jtroo's own commits, merged direct. This is a healthy two-tier pattern: external contributions go through formal review; maintainer internal changes go direct.

44% formal review across 300 PRs is the highest in the V1.2 catalog — compare to Xray-core (3.3%), wezterm (3.3%), kamal (4.7%), QuickLook (5.1%), browser_terminal (0%). The substantive co-maintainer (ItayGarin at 149 commits) shows up as an independent reviewer in the sample — this is real multi-reviewer activity, not self-review.

This finding exists to make the governance picture honest. F0 says 'no enforced protection'. F3 says 'the practice is healthy anyway'. Both are true. The recommendation for consumers is: if you see `jtroo/kanata` in a supply chain you depend on, the informal review culture is a real positive signal — just be aware that it's unenforceable (a compromised account bypasses it entirely, which F0 captures).

kanata's default install (`cargo install kanata`) is key-remap-only — configs can define layers, tap-hold actions, macros, and so on, but cannot execute shell commands. The `cmd` action is behind a Cargo feature flag: `cargo install kanata --features cmd`. Enabling it expands the daemon's capability to include running arbitrary shell commands triggered by config-defined actions.

This is good risk-reduction design: opt-in, documented, off by default. The finding exists to make the capability visible to readers. Consumers who enable `--features cmd` are signing up for a qualitatively different threat model — a kanata config becomes executable content. Third-party config files shared on the internet (a common practice in the keyboard-customization community) should be audited before loading when the `cmd` feature is enabled.

Two Rust-ecosystem scanner gaps — identical to wezterm entry 21: (1) Cargo.lock parsing not implemented, so runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 are coverage artifacts; (2) distribution-channel detection surfaced 12 crates.io entries from workspace sub-crates when only `crates-kanata` is the real consumer install path. Dangerous-primitives hits (3 total) are consistent with the Rust-serde / test-fixture false-positive class.

The pattern is now established — V1.2 Rust scans consistently hit the same three gaps. Consolidated V1.2.x harness patch candidate: Cargo.lock parsing + workspace-member disambiguation + serde regex tuning. Consumer-side: run `cargo audit` locally to fill the dep-CVE visibility gap.
